trust_assumption: A SaaS tenant that grants a third-party integration an OAuth scope reasons about that one app in isolation - 'App A may read my CRM' - and trusts that each sanctioned integration's authority is bounded by its own grant, so it never evaluates the transitive graph where App A's read scope plus App B's external-write scope compose into a read-then-exfiltrate primitive that neither grant alone implies.
violation: An attacker who compromises one low-sensitivity integration (or its long-lived token) chains it against a second sanctioned integration in the same tenant - reading sensitive records through App A and pushing them out through App B's outbound scope - so the combined capability exceeds any single consent screen the tenant ever approved, and because every hop uses a valid, sanctioned token the lateral movement is indistinguishable from normal SaaS-to-SaaS automation traffic.

techniques.name: Toxic-combination chaining of independently-granted OAuth scopes
Long-lived integration token replay for sanctioned-traffic lateral movement

rationale: The 2025 Salesloft-Drift OAuth breach showed that valid third-party integration tokens, replayed, can pivot into hundreds of downstream SaaS tenants while blending into sanctioned traffic, but the deeper unmodeled seam is that consent is granted per-app while authority composes across the integration mesh. No tenant consent UI, OAuth scope model, or SSPM control evaluates the transitive scope graph - the toxic combination of App A's read and App B's write - so a defender approving each integration individually cannot see the read-then-exfiltrate primitive their integration sprawl already implements. This is distinct from generic stolen-token replay because the violated trust is the implicit assumption that per-grant scope bounds per-grant authority; the attacker needs no scope escalation, only to compose grants the tenant itself installed. Detection that reasons over the cross-integration scope graph rather than per-app grants is nascent, and there is no transitive-scope governance standard.
suggested_research: Build a SaaS tenant scope-graph analyzer that enumerates installed integrations, models each app's effective read/write/identity scopes, and flags toxic compositions (read-sensitive in one app reachable to external-write in another); then red-team by chaining two benign-looking apps to demonstrate exfiltration that no single consent screen authorized, and propose a transitive-scope consent model.

trust_assumption: A SaaS application that exposes an inbound webhook endpoint trusts the event payload it receives as an authentic, authoritative statement of what happened in a partner system - 'payment.succeeded', 'user.verified', 'document.signed' - and lets that payload directly mutate its own state, treating data delivered over the webhook as a control signal rather than as an untrusted assertion to be re-verified against the source of truth.
violation: An attacker forges or replays a webhook event - exploiting an endpoint with no signature, a verifier that parses-then-verifies a re-canonicalized body, or a signature scheme with no timestamp/nonce binding - and the receiving SaaS acts on the payload's claim, flipping an entitlement, marking an order paid, or promoting an account, so attacker-supplied data crosses directly into the application's control plane without the receiver ever pulling current state back from the originating system.

techniques.name: Forged/replayed webhook event mutating receiver state
Payload-canonicalization HMAC bypass on inbound webhook

rationale: Webhooks are the connective tissue of the 2025-2027 integration economy, and the recommended-but-rarely-followed pattern is to treat each webhook as a trigger and re-fetch authoritative state from the source; in practice receivers trust the payload and mutate state directly, and signature verification is widely broken by missing timestamp/nonce binding, plaintext-echoed signing secrets, and parse-before-verify canonicalization bugs. The seam is a pure P1 data-to-control violation: an event payload, which is attacker-influenceable assertion, is promoted to an authoritative command over the receiver's business logic. This is distinct from generic API abuse and from the OAuth scope-chain gap because the trust object is the inbound event's truthfulness, not a credential or a scope graph - the attacker need not authenticate at all if signing is absent or replayable. There is no cross-SaaS standard for event provenance binding, and detection of forged-but-well-formed events that match expected schema is essentially absent.
suggested_research: Survey inbound-webhook handlers across popular SaaS for verify-then-trust-payload anti-patterns (no re-fetch, weak/absent signature, no replay window), build a forging harness that mutates receiver state via crafted events, and prototype a receiver-side gate that mandates source-of-truth re-fetch plus nonce+timestamp signature binding before any state-changing action.

trust_assumption: An iPaaS platform (Zapier/Make/Workato-style) trusts that any recipe or scenario built in a workspace may execute under the workspace's shared, pre-authorized connections, so a flow author inherits the full authority of every connection an admin once added - dozens of OAuth grants to business-critical systems - without the platform re-checking that this particular automation, author, or trigger was authorized for that specific connection's scope.
violation: An attacker who can author or edit a flow in the workspace (a compromised low-privilege builder account, an over-shared workspace, or a malicious imported template) attaches an existing privileged shared connection to a new automation and pivots its authority into systems they were never individually granted, so the connection's stored, long-lived OAuth tokens become an ambient capability any flow can borrow, and one workspace compromise inherits the union of every connected SaaS at once.

techniques.name: Shared-connection reuse by unauthorized iPaaS flow
Malicious imported recipe/template binding a privileged connection

rationale: iPaaS platforms centralize connections so authors do not re-authenticate per recipe, which means a single workspace holds the OAuth tokens for potentially dozens of business systems and any flow can borrow them - a blast radius security teams call toxic combinations. The unmodeled seam is context-inheritance: the platform binds authority to the workspace-level connection rather than to the specific automation+author+trigger, so authoring rights silently equal the union of all connection scopes. This is distinct from the SaaS-to-SaaS OAuth chain (which composes scopes across separate first-party integrations a tenant installed) because here a single multi-tenant automation broker holds a shared credential pool that any in-workspace flow inherits, and the entry point is flow-authoring rather than token theft. Per-connection authorization scoping, recipe-level least privilege, and detection of a flow attaching a connection it has no business using are largely absent across the major platforms, especially as iPaaS becomes the execution layer for agentic/MCP automation.
suggested_research: Map how leading iPaaS platforms scope shared connections to flows and authors, build a privilege-fanout analyzer that computes the effective reachable systems from a single workspace foothold, and demonstrate connection-borrowing by a low-privilege author or imported template; propose per-recipe connection binding and approval so authoring rights no longer equal the union of all connections.

trust_assumption: Every SaaS app a user is logged into in their browser implicitly trusts that requests carrying its session cookies/tokens originate from the user's own intent within the page, while a granted browser extension trusts that its declared host permissions entitle it to read and act inside those same authenticated origins, so an extension silently inherits the user's live, MFA-satisfied sessions to every site its permissions cover as an ambient, already-authenticated authority.
violation: An extension that turns malicious (silent post-install update, acquired/compromised developer account, or a sleeper bought after gaining users) uses its host-permission reach to read session cookies and bearer tokens and to drive in-page actions, impersonating the user across Microsoft 365, Workspace, and other SaaS without ever touching the login flow, so account takeover happens after authentication and entirely behind MFA, inheriting the trust the SaaS placed in the browser session.

techniques.name: Session-cookie/token theft via granted host permissions
Silent malicious extension update inheriting live sessions

rationale: 2025 campaigns (ShadyPanda, mass developer-account compromises, ChatGPT-session hijacks) confirmed that extensions with broad host permissions sit inside every authenticated origin and can lift session tokens to impersonate SaaS accounts entirely post-MFA. The unmodeled seam is context-inheritance across two trust planes at once: the SaaS trusts the browser session, and the browser trusts the extension's permissions, so the extension transitively inherits the user's authenticated authority to every covered site without re-consent. This is distinct from classic infostealer cookie theft on the endpoint because the trust object is the WebExtension permission grant itself as an authority-inheritance boundary - the user approved 'read data on sites you visit' once, and an update later weaponizes that standing grant against MFA-protected SaaS. Browser-side controls that isolate cookies from extensions are research-stage, enterprise extension governance is immature, and there is no detection that a session was acted on by an extension rather than the human.
suggested_research: Prototype an enterprise extension-risk monitor that diffs extension updates for newly-requested host/cookie permissions and flags extensions reading session tokens for SSO origins; red-team a sleeper extension that flips post-install to harvest M365/Workspace sessions, and propose a browser primitive that withholds SSO session cookies from extension contexts unless explicitly re-consented.

trust_assumption: An enterprise treats its telemetry pipeline - the OpenTelemetry/Datadog/Fluent collector running with broad host and network reach - as a trusted, benign plumbing component, so it is allowed to read logs/metrics/traces from everywhere and ship them to whatever exporter destinations its (often remotely-managed) config names, and the SOC trusts that whatever the pipeline delivers is the complete, unmanipulated record of what happened.
violation: An attacker who can push a collector config (via the OpAMP/remote-management channel, a poisoned config repo, or a modified collector image) adds an attacker-controlled exporter to turn the always-trusted, egress-permitted agent into a sanctioned exfiltration channel, and simultaneously inserts processors that drop or filter security-relevant signals, blinding the SOC during the operation - all while the pipeline's provenance as 'just observability' suppresses scrutiny of its destinations and omissions.

techniques.name: Rogue exporter added via collector remote-config (OpAMP) for egress
Telemetry-processor filter to silence security signal

rationale: Telemetry collectors are deployed fleet-wide with privileged read and broad egress, and increasingly take remote configuration over OpAMP, making the config channel and exporter destination set a high-value, under-watched control surface; a non-allowlisted exporter push is itself a high-confidence compromise indicator that few orgs alarm on. The unmodeled seam is provenance trust: the SOC trusts the pipeline to faithfully and completely relay reality, so an attacker who controls config can both exfiltrate through the egress-blessed agent and delete the evidence of doing so in the same move. This is distinct from generic log tampering and from EDR-disable because the trust object is the observability pipeline's role as the authoritative, benign, egress-permitted relay - it is simultaneously a sanctioned data-out channel and the system that would otherwise witness the attack. There is little enforcement that the deployed exporter set matches an allowlist, and detection that the pipeline itself has started dropping or rerouting security telemetry is nascent.
suggested_research: Build a collector-config attestation and exporter-allowlist enforcement layer that alarms on any new destination or signal-dropping processor, run a dedicated SOC-pipeline collector with no remote-management surface, and red-team the OpAMP channel to demonstrate combined exfiltration-plus-blinding while measuring how long the SOC stays dark.

trust_assumption: A calendar-integrated AI meeting bot trusts that any event on a user's calendar is a legitimate invitation to attend, record, and transcribe, so it auto-joins on the user's behalf and inherits a standing presence in conversations; downstream, the org trusts the bot's transcript/summary as a faithful record and lets it flow into CRM notes, action items, and agent workflows as authoritative meeting truth.
violation: An attacker injects a calendar event (via an open invite, a spoofed organizer, or a compromised scheduling integration) to summon a victim's note-taker bot into a meeting it was never meant to attend - silently exfiltrating the conversation - or seeds the meeting/transcript with content engineered so the AI summary and extracted action items carry attacker-chosen instructions or false 'agreed' decisions into the downstream systems that ingest the bot's output as trusted record.

techniques.name: Calendar-injection summoning auto-join bot for covert capture
Transcript/summary poisoning into downstream agent workflows

rationale: Auto-join note-takers (Otter, Fireflies, Fathom and peers) are pervasive 2025-2026 shadow IT - they attend on calendar triggers and route summaries into CRMs and downstream automations - and the 2025 Otter class action highlighted the consent/capture exposure of silent attendance. The unmodeled seam is provenance: the bot's presence is authorized by the calendar (an attacker-writable surface via open/spoofed invites) and its transcript is later trusted as an authoritative neutral record by humans and by agent pipelines that act on the extracted decisions. This is distinct from generic phishing and from prompt-injection-into-RAG because the trust objects are specifically (a) a calendar event as authorization for autonomous bot presence and (b) an AI meeting summary as ground-truth organizational record feeding action automation - a two-stage seam unique to the meeting-bot economy. There is no provenance signal that a transcript item was attacker-seeded, and no governance that gates which calendar events may summon a recording identity.
suggested_research: Demonstrate calendar-injection that summons a target's note-taker into an unintended meeting and quantify covert capture; separately seed a meeting with content that steers the AI summary's action items, and trace whether downstream CRM/agent automations execute the injected decisions - then propose invite-provenance gating for auto-join and provenance tagging on summary-derived actions.

trust_assumption: Enterprise systems trust that actions performed under a user identity reflect that human's intent and presence, while RPA platforms run attended and unattended bots that hold and replay human or human-equivalent credentials to drive those same systems through the UI, so target applications grant the bot the full authority of the impersonated identity and attribute its every action to a person who was not there.
violation: An attacker who compromises the RPA orchestrator, a bot's credential vault, or an unattended runner harvests the standing human-equivalent credentials and drives privileged business actions through the bot's sanctioned UI automation, and because the target system attributes the activity to the human identity the bot impersonates, the malicious actions are non-repudiably blamed on an innocent user and blend into expected automated workflow traffic.

techniques.name: RPA orchestrator/vault compromise harvesting bot credentials
Attended-bot impersonation attributing actions to the human

rationale: RPA fleets impersonate humans by design - attended bots run under user accounts with no isolation from the controlling user, and unattended bots hold elevated credentials to perform privileged operations - making the credential store and orchestrator a concentrated, high-value identity cache, and vendor guidance itself flags credential misuse as the top RPA risk. The unmodeled seam is identity-to-authority with a non-repudiation twist: the target application cannot distinguish bot-driven from human-driven actions, so a compromised bot both wields the human's authority and launders attribution onto that human. This is distinct from generic stolen-credential use and from orphaned-NHI gaps because the bot is a deliberately human-impersonating actor with no second factor of presence, sitting between the credential vault and the UI it drives; the attacker inherits not just access but the victim's accountability. Behavioral separation of bot vs human actions, and detection of a bot acting outside its normal recipe, remain weak as RPA expands and fuses with AI agents.
suggested_research: Prototype an identity-attribution layer that distinguishes RPA-driven from human-driven sessions (signed bot context, presence factors) so target systems can authorize and audit bot actions distinctly; red-team an orchestrator/vault compromise to show authority capture plus attribution laundering, and define a non-repudiation standard for automated identities.

trust_assumption: A business process trusts the 'agreement completed/signed' signal from an e-signature workflow as a final, authoritative legal fact, so the completion event - delivered by callback or read from the platform - is wired to release value automatically: provisioning access, paying an invoice, triggering the next agreement, or archiving an authoritative copy, on the assumption that a completion event means the right parties validly signed the right unaltered document.
violation: An attacker subverts the completion signal rather than the cryptographic seal - forging or replaying the completion webhook, manipulating the signer-routing/recipient logic so an unauthorized party satisfies the signing step, or racing a document/template swap before the downstream system fetches the 'completed' artifact - so the receiving system releases value on a completion that is procedurally invalid even though each tamper-sealed page checks out, exploiting the gap between 'a completion event arrived' and 'the intended parties validly agreed to this content'.

techniques.name: Forged/replayed e-signature completion callback releasing value
Signer-routing/recipient-logic abuse to satisfy signing step

rationale: E-signature platforms emphasize PKI tamper-seals on the document, but the integration economy increasingly wires the completion event itself - via Connect-style webhooks and workflow builders that chain a second agreement or fire an ERP invoice on completion - into automatic value release, and that event channel is where trust is misplaced. The unmodeled seam is provenance of the completion fact as distinct from integrity of the document bytes: the downstream system treats 'completed' as proof the right parties validly agreed, while routing logic, recipient substitution, or a forged/replayed completion callback can produce a 'completed' state that the tamper-seal never contradicts. This is distinct from the generic inbound-webhook gap because the trust object is specifically the legal completion attestation that gates automated value transfer, and distinct from document-tamper attacks because the seal stays valid - the procedural validity, not the bytes, is forged. There is no downstream control that re-verifies signer identity and routing provenance before acting on completion, and no detection of value released on a procedurally-invalid completion.
suggested_research: Map e-signature completion-to-action integrations (callback trust, signer-routing flexibility, race windows before artifact fetch) and demonstrate value release on a forged/replayed or mis-routed completion whose document seal remains valid; propose a downstream gate that re-verifies signer identity, routing, and a nonce-bound completion attestation against the platform API before releasing value.

trust_assumption: A low-code/no-code platform trusts that a flow built by any citizen developer may run under whatever connection reference is attached to it, so when an admin shares a privileged service connection (or a flow itself) into a maker environment, every automation that references it executes with that connection's elevated authority on the assumption that the maker was implicitly entitled to it.
violation: A citizen developer (or an attacker who has taken over a maker account, or who plants a shared flow/template) authors an automation that references an admin's privileged shared connection and uses it to perform actions far beyond their own rights - reading restricted data, writing to production systems, or creating identities - so the platform silently elevates a low-privilege author to the connection owner's authority, and the action is logged as the service connection rather than the human who actually directed it.

techniques.name: Citizen-dev flow referencing a shared privileged connection
Shared/imported flow persisting attacker logic on a privileged connector

rationale: Low-code/no-code platforms (Power Platform, App-builder and flow tools) let business users compose automations against shared connections, and the prevalent failure is that authority is bound to the connection reference, not to the author or the action - so a privileged connection shared into an environment becomes ambient elevation for anyone who can attach it. The unmodeled seam is context-inheritance: a citizen-dev flow inherits the owning service connection's privilege without the platform re-authorizing the specific author for that scope, and audit attributes the action to the connection rather than the person. This is distinct from the iPaaS shared-connection gap (a third-party automation broker holding a multi-system token pool) because here the elevation happens inside the enterprise's own first-party low-code tenant via citizen developers and shared/imported flows, and distinct from RPA bots because there is no impersonation runner - the privilege leaks through the connection-reference model itself. As these platforms become persistence-friendly hosting for agentic logic, attacker-planted shared flows on privileged connectors are a quiet foothold, and governance/detection for connection-borrowing by under-entitled makers is largely absent.
suggested_research: Analyze how low-code platforms bind execution authority to shared connections versus authors, build a maker-environment privilege-leak analyzer that flags flows referencing connections beyond the author's own rights, and demonstrate elevation and persistence via a shared/imported flow on a privileged connector; propose per-flow connection authorization and human-attributed auditing of connector-driven actions.
